title: SCORE — Assistant Manager / Manager Assessment
audience: Assistant Managers and Managers overseeing BIM coordination teams
description: Purely HR/behavioral instrument, 30 questions at ~60 seconds each. Technical/software skill is assessed separately (technical test + Personal Interview), so this test carries no technical items at all. Weighted for a managerial role: Leadership gets the most questions, Communication and Team Play next, then Problem-Solving; Dispute Resolution and Organizational Mindset carry smaller dedicated quotas (though still higher than at the modeler level, reflecting real managerial exposure), and Ethics is a minimal but always-active dimension — ethics-concern flags can surface on any question, not just the 2 dedicated ones.

sections.instructions: Choose the option that best reflects how you would actually act. There is no single 'correct' answer — pick the one truest to your instinct.

questions.prompt: A junior staff member makes a significant error that reaches the client before being caught. As team lead, what's your response?

options.text: Quietly fix it and never mention it to the client to avoid embarrassment.
Blame the junior staff member to the client to protect yourself.
Own it with the client, communicate the correction and prevention plan, and address the root cause privately and constructively with the staff member.
Terminate the junior staff member immediately.

questions.prompt: A major clash is discovered right before a permit submission and your team is panicking. What's your leadership approach?

options.text: Take over every task yourself to make sure it's done right.
Stay calm, quickly triage the issue, delegate clear ownership of specific parts, and set a tight but realistic timeline with check-ins.
Let the team figure it out without your involvement, to avoid micromanaging.
Focus on identifying who's at fault before solving the technical problem.

questions.prompt: A talented team member consistently disrespects deadlines but produces excellent technical work. How do you manage this?

options.text: Overlook the deadline issue entirely because the work quality is high.
Address the pattern directly and specifically, set clear expectations and consequences, while acknowledging their strength.
Criticize them publicly in front of the team.
Quietly reassign their tasks to others without discussing it with them.

questions.prompt: A new Assistant Manager under you is eager but making decisions above their authority level, causing confusion on the team. How do you handle it?

options.text: Publicly undermine their decisions in front of the team.
Have a direct, supportive conversation clarifying decision boundaries, mentor them on judgment, and reinforce it consistently.
Say nothing and quietly override their decisions when you notice them.
Remove their decision-making authority entirely without explanation.

questions.prompt: Productivity data (billed hours vs. deliverables) shows one team member underperforming for several months. What's your approach as manager?

options.text: Reduce that person's billed hours retroactively without discussing it with them or the client.
Have a private, evidence-based performance conversation, identify root causes, set a clear improvement plan with support, and follow up.
Let it continue to avoid a difficult conversation.
Reassign all their work to others without ever discussing performance with them directly.

questions.prompt: You're promoted to manage former peers. One of them resents reporting to you now. What do you do?

options.text: Avoid giving them any direction to keep the peace.
Have an honest, direct conversation acknowledging the shift, clarify expectations, and earn credibility through fair, consistent leadership.
Assert your authority forcefully to establish dominance early.
Report their attitude to your own manager immediately without addressing it yourself.

questions.prompt: A high-priority client project needs a lead assigned from your team, and two capable people want the role. How do you decide?

options.text: Pick whoever asks most persistently.
Assess based on project fit, availability, and development opportunity, communicate the reasoning transparently to both, and offer the other a clear next opportunity.
Avoid deciding and let them sort it out between themselves.
Choose based on personal preference without explaining the reasoning.

questions.prompt: A client is unhappy about slipped deadlines and threatens to escalate to your director. What's your approach?

options.text: Avoid the client's calls until you have better news.
Proactively reach out, acknowledge the issue honestly, present a clear recovery plan with realistic dates, and update them regularly.
Attribute the delay entirely to subcontractors/other disciplines when speaking with the client.
Promise an unrealistic recovery date just to calm the client down.

questions.prompt: A client asks directly whether your team can meet an aggressive deadline you privately doubt is realistic. What do you say?

options.text: Say yes to avoid disappointing them, and figure it out later.
Give an honest assessment of the risks and what would need to be true to hit the date, then propose options.
Say no immediately without offering alternatives.
Avoid giving a direct answer.

questions.prompt: A client says your own communication style in status meetings is 'too technical and hard to follow.' How do you respond?

options.text: Dismiss it — technical accuracy matters more than simplicity.
Take the feedback seriously, adapt your approach for that audience, and check in for ongoing feedback.
Get defensive and explain why the client should follow your explanations more closely.
Delegate all future client meetings to someone else without addressing the issue yourself.

questions.prompt: Budget cuts mean reducing your team's software license count, which will slow some work. How do you communicate this internally?

options.text: Don't explain the reasoning — just enforce the cut.
Explain the business context honestly, involve the team in prioritizing affected workflows, and set realistic expectations with stakeholders.
Blame 'management' vaguely without owning the decision as a leader.
Delay telling the team until problems arise from the change.

questions.prompt: You need to deliver difficult news to the whole team — a valued project was cancelled or scope significantly reduced. How do you approach it?

options.text: Send a brief email and avoid further discussion.
Hold a live conversation, explain context honestly, acknowledge the impact, and answer questions directly.
Let it leak out informally rather than addressing it directly.
Downplay the significance to avoid concern, even if that means being misleading.

questions.prompt: Two people on your team interpret your instructions for a task completely differently, causing duplicated/conflicting effort. What do you do?

options.text: Blame them for not asking enough questions.
Take ownership of the miscommunication, clarify immediately, and adjust your future instructions to be more explicit.
Let them sort out the confusion themselves.
Pick whichever interpretation is more convenient and move forward without explaining.

questions.prompt: Two previously siloed discipline sub-teams under you rarely collaborate and duplicate effort. How do you build a stronger sense of shared team identity?

options.text: Leave it as is since each sub-team still delivers its own work.
Create shared touchpoints (joint reviews, shared goals/recognition) and actively model cross-team collaboration yourself.
Merge them formally without addressing the underlying culture issue.
Address it only if a client complains about the disconnect.

questions.prompt: A star performer on your team refuses to mentor or share knowledge with others, protecting their own indispensability. What do you do?

options.text: Leave them alone since their individual output is excellent.
Have a direct conversation linking knowledge-sharing to how you evaluate senior-level contribution, and create structured opportunities for them to mentor.
Publicly criticize them for not being a team player.
Quietly route more mentoring responsibility to others without addressing the root behavior.

questions.prompt: Your team must absorb extra scope with no extra time, and morale is already stretched. How do you rally them?

options.text: Just assign the extra work and expect people to manage.
Be transparent about the situation, involve the team in deciding how to divide the load fairly, and acknowledge the extra effort.
Take on all the extra work yourself without asking the team.
Promise rewards you're not sure you can deliver to motivate them.

questions.prompt: A remote or satellite-office team member feels excluded from key decisions made in the main office. What do you do?

options.text: Assume it's a minor issue since they still receive the final decisions.
Actively include them in relevant discussions going forward and check in on how included they feel.
Suggest they raise it themselves if it's really bothering them.
Address it only if it starts affecting their output.

questions.prompt: A Junior and a Senior on the same task are competitive with each other rather than collaborative, and it's affecting quality. What do you do?

options.text: Let them work it out since some competition can be healthy.
Set clear shared goals and structure the task so their success is interdependent, addressing the competitive dynamic directly.
Separate them onto different tasks without addressing the dynamic.
Reprimand both without understanding the underlying dynamic.

questions.prompt: A team lead under you wants to skip a QA review step to hit a deadline, 'just this once.' What's your call?

options.text: Approve skipping QA — deadlines matter and it's a one-time thing.
Say no, but work with the team to find a faster path to complete QA properly rather than skipping it.
Approve it without discussion since you trust the lead's judgment.
Say no without offering any alternative path to still meet the deadline.

questions.prompt: A client's facilities team insists on a design change that conflicts with a code-compliance issue your BIM team identified. What do you do?

options.text: Implement the client's requested change since they're paying for the project.
Clearly and formally communicate the code conflict and risk, get the decision documented, and escalate internally and to the client's leadership before implementing anything non-compliant.
Implement the change but have a junior document it quietly as a CYA measure, without informing the client formally.
Refuse to discuss it further with the client.

questions.prompt: Your team can hit the deadline OR maintain full quality, but not both, and the client hasn't been made aware of this trade-off. What's your approach as manager?

options.text: Quietly cut quality to hit the deadline without telling the client.
Present the trade-off honestly to the client and let them decide which matters more, or negotiate additional time/resource.
Miss the deadline without any communication in advance.
Push the team to work unsustainable hours to try to hit both, without addressing the systemic issue.

questions.prompt: Two competing internal priorities — a strategic new client and an existing client's ongoing work — both need your best people at the same time. How do you resolve the resourcing conflict?

options.text: Give the new client whatever they want since they're strategically important.
Evaluate objectively against business priorities, communicate trade-offs transparently to both stakeholders, and look for creative resourcing options before choosing.
Default to whichever client complains loudest.
Split your best people evenly regardless of actual project needs.

questions.prompt: A long-standing process at your studio clearly isn't working anymore as the studio has grown, but changing it will be disruptive short-term. What do you do?

options.text: Leave it alone since change is disruptive and risky.
Propose and pilot a change with a clear plan to manage the short-term disruption, gathering team input first.
Force the change immediately without piloting or consulting anyone.
Complain about the process being outdated without proposing anything concrete.

questions.prompt: Two department leads under you have an ongoing personal conflict that's starting to affect team morale and coordination quality. What do you do?

options.text: Avoid getting involved since it's a personal matter.
Meet with each separately, then together, understand root causes, establish clear working agreements, and follow up over time.
Publicly reprimand both in a team meeting.
Transfer one of them immediately without addressing the root issue.

questions.prompt: A dispute escalates between your team and another team (led by a peer manager) over who's responsible for a coordination failure that affected the client. What do you do?

options.text: Publicly blame the other team to protect your own team's reputation.
Meet privately with the peer manager first to understand both sides, focus on resolving the root cause and preventing recurrence rather than assigning blame, then align on a joint message.
Avoid engaging with the other manager and let the client draw their own conclusions.
Escalate immediately to senior leadership without attempting to resolve it peer-to-peer first.

questions.prompt: Two of your direct reports have a disagreement about technical approach that's becoming personal rather than professional. What do you do?

options.text: Let them work it out, since you don't want to seem like you're taking sides.
Intervene early, separate the technical disagreement from the personal friction, mediate a resolution on the technical point, and address the interpersonal tension directly.
Side with whichever one is more senior to end the disagreement quickly.
Reassign one of them to a different task to avoid dealing with the conflict.

questions.prompt: You notice a pattern where multiple projects at your studio hit avoidable delays for the same root-cause reason. What's your response as manager?

options.text: Handle each project's delay individually as it comes up without addressing the pattern.
Raise the systemic pattern with studio leadership, propose a root-cause fix, and track whether it actually improves outcomes across projects.
Assume other managers will notice and raise it themselves.
Mention it informally in passing without pursuing a real fix.

questions.prompt: A decision that's good for your team's short-term numbers would be mildly harmful to the studio's reputation with a client relationship that matters long-term. What do you do?

options.text: Prioritize your team's short-term numbers since that's what you're measured on.
Weigh the long-term studio/client relationship appropriately, even if it means a less favorable short-term outcome for your own team's metrics, and explain the reasoning upward.
Make the short-term-favorable choice without considering or mentioning the long-term angle.
Avoid making the decision and hope it resolves itself.

questions.prompt: Senior management asks you to reduce hours reported to a client without reducing the actual work required, to preserve a fee cap. What do you do?

options.text: Comply, since it's a direction from senior management.
Push back clearly, explain the ethical/legal risk of misreporting billed hours, and propose alternatives (scope renegotiation, efficiency review).
Comply, but keep a private record for yourself in case of future questions.
Ignore the instruction without addressing it directly with management.

questions.prompt: You discover a deliverable sent to a client last week contained a significant, uncaught modeling error affecting cost estimates. What do you do?

options.text: Wait to see if the client notices before saying anything.
Proactively notify the client as soon as possible, explain the correction, and review internal QA to prevent recurrence.
Correct it quietly in the next deliverable without flagging the earlier error.
Attribute the error to the software or an external vendor when informing the client.
